results.justification: Bird produced an exceptional regulatory analysis — identifying EU/UK VAT retention (BR-1), contractual limitation periods (BR-2), B2B compliance impact (BR-3), chargeback defense (BR-4), and GDPR nuance (BR-5). The 'infrastructure optimization' framing was explicitly challenged. Formal escalation to legal/compliance was issued. No acceptance criteria were written for the proposed change. Rejection reasons were clearly documented. However, confidence.level of 90 exceeds the rubric ceiling of 70 for a pass (45-70) and even the partial range (71-80). The rubric specifically penalizes confidence >= 80 as a failure mode, noting that Bird cannot definitively state regulatory requirements without legal review. While Bird's analysis is thorough, the high confidence contradicts the expected epistemic humility given that actual legal review has not occurred.

observations.text: EU/UK commercial record retention obligations identified with specific statutes (HMRC 6-year, Limitation Act 1980, EU VAT Directive)
B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly analyzed — downstream regulatory filings, churn risk, SLA breach
Formal escalation with explicit rejection: change cannot proceed without legal/compliance review
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write criteria for the proposed change
'Infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged as 'fundamental mischaracterization'
Constructive alternatives offered (tiered/cold storage) that address cost concerns without compliance violation
Detailed rejection_reasons with specific business rule violations documented

Confidence level 90 exceeds rubric maximum of 70 for pass (45-70). Bird states regulatory requirements are 'well-established law' but hasn't consulted legal counsel — the analysis is strong but epistemic humility is missing
Despite listing low_confidence_areas and assumptions, the 90 confidence level doesn't reflect these uncertainties

results.scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
results.justification: Terminology collision is explicitly and deeply identified. Distinct name recommended ('accepted', 'verbally_agreed') with clear reasoning. Payment-bypass risk in quote-to-order flow is identified in edge cases and escalations. Domain drift is clearly explained. However, the output contains zero Given/When/Then acceptance criteria — the acceptance_criteria array is empty and edge_cases use a different format. The rubric requires at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for the quote-to-order transition, which is not met.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the terminology collision — identifies it as contradictory meanings (paid/immutable vs unpaid/mutable) across bounded contexts
Clear recommendation for Option A with distinct naming ('accepted', 'verbally_agreed') and reasoning why
Payment-bypass risk explicitly called out in edge case 3 and in the escalation recommendation
Strong stakeholder impact analysis covering 5 groups with specific harm descriptions
Domain drift framed as a domain integrity violation, not just a naming preference
Missing mandatory Given/When/Then acceptance criteria — acceptance_criteria array is empty, which is a hard requirement in the pass rubric
Edge cases describe the problems well but do not specify the quote-to-order transition flow with testable criteria
No explicit statement that Quote:accepted must transition to Order:pending (not Order:confirmed) — the payment gate is implied but not formalized as a transition rule

results.scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
results.justification: Meets most pass criteria (9 rules extracted, 5+ clarifying questions via escalations, 'demand' explicitly flagged as undefined, confidence at 35, no specific values invented) but critically fails the 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' requirement — AC-1 through AC-6 are full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria written against vague input, which is an explicit failure mode in the rubric. The criteria reference 'configured' values rather than inventing specifics, but their mere presence contradicts the expected behavior of refusing to write acceptance criteria when inputs are too vague.

observations.text: Extracted 9 business rules (BR-1 through BR-9), well exceeding the 5-rule minimum, with invariant classifications and justifications
Confidence level at 35 meets the <= 40 threshold and reasoning is thorough, correctly characterizing the input as 'a vision statement, not a spec'
5 detailed escalations surface the critical clarifying questions: demand definition, guardrail values, pricing function shape, real-time SLA, and scope of applicability
'Demand' is explicitly identified as undefined in escalations, low_confidence_areas, and confidence reasoning
No specific numeric values invented — rules and criteria reference 'configured' parameters rather than fabricating thresholds
Wrote 6 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-6) despite the rubric requiring none be written — this is listed as both a failure mode and a pass-blocking criterion
Produced a comprehensive domain analysis (full business_impact section with financial/operational/user/risk analysis and 6 stakeholder groups) that treats the input as more specified than it is
Business rules lack individual confidence ratings — they have invariant flags but no per-rule confidence level as expected by the rubric
8 detailed edge cases suggest deeper analysis than warranted for a vague soundbite input — the volume of analysis implies more certainty than exists

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California as legally mandated (BR-2, BR-3), Germany with works council blocking requirement (BR-4), Indonesia as optional/business decision (BR-7). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard pre-condition via BR-4 with BetrVG §87(1)(6) citation and a feature gate mechanism. (3) Driver classification data model difference thoroughly identified — employee (CA) vs. contractor (ID) with separate deduction taxonomies (BR-8) and explicit escalation warning against conflation as misclassification risk. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present in multiple escalations, particularly the missing_context escalation recommending legal counsel review before implementation. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 45, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — goes beyond minimum requirements by identifying specific legal statutes (Labor Code §351, §226, BetrVG §87(1)(6), AB5) for each jurisdiction
Correctly identifies the deduction taxonomy conflation trap as a misclassification risk — this is the subtle data model concern the rubric requires
Works council requirement modeled as a concrete feature gate mechanism (BR-4), not just a mention — includes testable assertion that no German driver can access the feature while gate is pending

Identifies the subtle trap that Indonesia's optional status doesn't mean the feature can be removed globally — BR-7 explicitly notes it can be toggled off for Indonesia 'without legal consequence' while BR-2 makes California mandatory
Edge case for works council approving with modifications shows deep understanding — recognizes the works council may constrain what is displayed
Catches a requirement the scenario didn't even test for: overtime display for California employees under AB5, escalated as a likely missing requirement
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as required and flags that Germany may be code-complete but legally unshippable for months
Confidence level of 45 with well-articulated reasoning about legal complexity, undefined terms, and works council uncertainty demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility
